tate of Califorrtia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
T.chnical "''''otance 
(916) 322·5602 
Adminiltrofion 
322·5660 
Assemblyman Patrick J. Nolan 
State Capitol, Rm. 2137 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Assemblyman Nolan: 
Eucutiv./l.....,al 
322·,5901 
Enfarcement 
322~1 
November 27, 1984 
Statements of Economic f nf.r.11 
322-64U 
Re: 
Our Advice No. A-84-293 
Commissioner Stanford has referred your October 18, 1984, 
letter regarding air travel arranged for you by SONY Corporation 
on August 16, 1984. 
I have several comments on your letter. 
First, you have apparently considered the air travel 
provided by SONY as a gift. 
While that is consistent with 
Commission opinions and advice, the Commission may reconsider 
this next year when it reviews its regulations and advice 
concerning treatment of all types of officeholder related 
expenses under the Political Reform Act. 
Second, under Government Code Section 82028(b) (2) I 
a gift to 
a public official may be returned to the donor within 30 days of 
receipt. 
The Commission has consistently held that paying the 
donor the fair market value for a "gift" 
has the same effect as 
returning the gift. 
Thus, assuming that the air travel 
consituted a gift, if you had paid SONY the fair market value of 
the air travel within 30 days of receipt (by September 16, 
1984), you would not have received a gift from SONY. 
However, 
you did not pay SONY until October 16, 1984, 60 days after 
receipt of the air travel. 
October 16 was too late to "return" 
the gift. 
Third, the CommissiQn has set forth guidelines on how to 
determine the value of a gift of air travel in its opinion 
issued to Peter G. Stone, 3 FPPC Opinions 52 (No. 77-003, 
June 9, 1977). 
In that opinion the Commission held that the 
fair market value of private air transportation is either the 
commercial air rate for a similar trip or the charter rate for 
such a trip divided by the number of passengers on the trip. 
The actual cost to the donor of the air travel is not the fair 
market value of the travel. 


Assemblyman Patrick J. Nolan 
November 27, 1984 
Page 2 
Finally, receipt of a gift of over $250 could require your 
disqualification under Government Code Section 87100 from a 
legislative decision which affected the donor differently than 
the public generally. 
In this case, disqualification would only 
be a possibility if the fair market value of the gift from SONY 
was $250 or more, and if the legislative decision involved 
"special" legislation which singled out SONY for special 
treatment. 
Legislation generally affecting all companies which 
manufacture the same types of goods as SONY could not require 
disqualification. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. 
I hope that this letter has answered your questions under 
the Political Reform Act concerning the air travel provided for 
you by SONY. 
If you have any further questions, please let me 
know. 
BAM:KM 
( 
Barbara A. Milman 
General Counsel 


PO. aoX~1I 
GLENDALE. CA 91201J

........... """"islaiure 
October 18, 1984 
Mr. Dan Stanford, Executive Director 
California Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Mr. Stanford: 
COMMITTEES 
REVENUE & TAXATION 
WAYS & MEANS 
HUMAN SERVICES 
SPECIAl ASSEMBl Y COMMITTEE 
POLICY RESEARCH MANAGEME!, 
COMMITTEE 
A representative of your commission contacted my office and 
expressed concern about a flight arranged by the Sony 
Corporation of America to allow me to return to the Capitol 
for an unexpected Ways and Means hearing on August 16, 1984. 
I have paid Sony Corporation the cost of the flight, $2,818.80, 
and am aware of no actual or potential conflict of interest 
resulting from their courtesy. 
I have been advised by my legal counsel, Charles H. Bell, Jr. 
of Nielsen, Hodgson, Parrinello & Mueller, that no disqualifi-
cation is or was required under either the Legislative Ethics 
Code or the Political Reform Act. 
If you have any questions regarding this matter please do 
not hesitate to call me at my office. 
PJN: 11: j 1 
Enclosures 
NOT PRINTED OR MAILED AT TAXPAYERS EXPENSE

sections.question_synthetic: Whether air travel provided by Sony Corporation to Assemblyman Nolan constitutes a reportable gift under the Political Reform Act, and what the implications are for disqualification from legislative decisions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that while the air travel may be considered a gift, Assemblyman Nolan's payment to Sony was untimely (60 days after receipt rather than within the 30-day return period), and disqualification would only apply if the gift's fair market value exceeded $250 and involved special legislation benefiting Sony specifically.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (Nolan's October 18, 1984 letter) and the FPPC's response letter (November 27, 1984). Primary analysis focuses on the FPPC response. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers present; question and conclusion synthesized from letter content. Letter references FPPC Opinion No. 77-003 (Stone case) for air travel valuation methodology.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether air travel provided by Sony Corporation to Assemblyman Nolan constitutes a reportable gift under the Political Reform Act, and what the implications are for disqualification from legislative decisions.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advised that while the air travel may be considered a gift, Assemblyman Nolan's payment to Sony was untimely (60 days after receipt rather than within the 30-day return period), and disqualification would only apply if the gift's fair market value exceeded $250 and involved special legislation benefiting Sony specifically.

embedding.summary: FPPC General Counsel Barbara Milman responds to Assemblyman Nolan's October 18, 1984 letter regarding air travel provided by Sony Corporation, addressing gift treatment, timely return provisions, valuation methods, and potential disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act.
